content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

August 11, 1982


Leo Ziffren, Esq.
Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher
2029 Century Park East, 41st Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90067

Re: Your Telephone Request for Advice
     Our No. A-82-145

Dear Mr. Ziffren:

     Thank you for your recent telephone requests for advice
regarding the reporting requirements under the Political
Reform Act for activities to influence legislative action.

     As we understand them, the facts are as follows: Your
client is a company which does not employ a lobbyist. The
company is a member of a trade association which does employ
a lobbyist. Legislation has been introduced which would
have a significant impact upon the company. Employees of
the company wish to send telegrams to their state legislators
urging specific action on this legislation. You have asked
what filing and reporting obligations would arise if the
company reimbursed its employees for the cost of the tele-
grams, which you initially estimated at $60-100 total.

     Our advice to you was that there would be no filing or
reporting obligations on the part of the company unless or
until it met the standards set forth in Government Code
Section 86108.1/ (employing a lobbyist or spending $2,500 to
influence legislative action in a calendar quarter). Since
the company is not an employer of a lobbyist (2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18618, copy enclosed) by virtue of its partici-
pation in the trade association, the $2,500 test is the
appropriate one in these circumstances.

     You have subsequently advised us that the company may
expend more than $2,500 in this calendar quarter in supporting
this attempt to influence legislative action. Consequently,

_____________________
1/  All statutory references made are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Leo Ziffren, Esq.
August 11, 1982
Page Two


you have asked our general advice as to what reporting obli-
gations may occur and when such reports would be due.

     Initially, the company should be concerned with account-
ing for its payments for or in connection with "influencing
legislative action."  Under Section 82032, this term is
defined to include:

     ... promoting, supporting, influencing, modifying,
     opposing or delaying any legislative ... action by any
     means, including but not limited to the provision or
     use of information, statistics, studies or analyses.

     Payments to influence legislative action include, inter alia,

     ... compensation, payment or reimbursement for the
     services, time or expenses of an employee, for or in
     connection with direct communication with any elective
     state official, legislative official or agency official.

                                        Section 82045(d).

     Clearly, the company's reimbursement of its employees' costs
for sending telegrams to their legislators falls within this
definition and those payments would count toward the $2,500
reporting threshold.  Section 82045 also delineates other
types of payments which qualify as "payments to influence,"
among them are:

     1.  Payments to or in support of a lobbyist;

     2.  Payments which directly benefit state officials
         (i.e., gifts, entertaining, etc.);

     3.  Payments for or in connection with soliciting or
         urging others to communicate directly with state
         officials regarding legislation.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted
regulations to further clarify what must be reported by
those who make payments of $2,500 or more in a calendar
quarter to influence legislative action.  One of those
regulations is 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620 (copy enclosed).
Among the provisions of the regulation, the subdivisions
which appear to be relevant to your client's situation are
the following:

Leo Zifgren, Esq.
August 11, 1982
Page Three


     (a)(3)  Other payments to influence legislative
or administrative action, specifically, payments
for or in connection with:

                         * * *

     (B)  Direct communication with a legis-
lative, agency or elective state official for
the primary purpose of influencing legislative
or administrative action; and

     (C)  Soliciting or urging persons other
than the filer or the filer's employees to
enter into direct communication with a legis-
lative, agency or elective state official for
the primary purpose of influencing legislative
or administrative action.

                         * * *

     (d)  Other payments to influence legislative or
administrative action.

     (1)  Compensation of employees other than
lobbyists.  All filers shall report a proportionate
share of the compensation paid to employees
other than lobbyists who are engaged for 10 percent
or more of their compensated time in or in connec-
tion with any of the activities described in
subsection (a)(3) above.  Such employees include
those providing secretarial and research services
and those preparing materials to be used by a
lobbyist or to be used in direct communication or
in soliciting or urging others to engage in direct
communication for the primary purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action.  With respect
to such compensation:

     (A)  The filer shall itemize by name and
title those employees whose proportionate
reportable compensation is $1,000 or more
during a month.

     (B)  The filer shall report in one lump
sum the compensation paid to all employees
whose proportionate reportable compensation
is less than $1,000 during a month.

Leo Ziffren, Esq.
August 11, 1982
Page Four

(C) Compensation which must be reported
includes gross wages paid plus any benefits
which are in lieu of wages such as the grant-
ing of stock options or the purchase of
annuities. It does not include, however,
routine fringe benefits, such as the employer's
contribution to health plans, retirement plans,
etc., which are made on behalf of all employees
nor does it include the payment of the employer's
payroll taxes.

(2) Payments other than compensation. In
addition to compensation, the periodic reports of
a filer shall include:

* * *

(C) The payment of any other expense
which would not have been incurred but for
the filer's activities to influence or attempt
to influence legislative or administrative
action, i.e., those activities described in
subsection (a)(3) above.

(e) Exceptions.

(1) Except for payments to lobbyists and the
compensation of employees other than lobbyists
which must be reported under subsection (d)(1)
above, a filer need not report any regular, ongoing
business overhead which would continue to be in-
curred in substantially similar amounts regardless
of the filer's activities to influence legislative
or administrative action.

(2) A filer does not have to report any part
of the costs of producing a regularly published
newsletter or periodical whose circulation is
limited to an organization's members, employees,
shareholders, other affiliated individuals and
those who request or purchase the publication.
This exception applies only to those costs regularly
incurred in publishing and distributing the news-
letter or periodical. If additional costs are
incurred because the newsletter or periodical is
issued on other than its regular schedule, expanded
in circulation, or substantially altered in style,
size or format for the primary purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence legislative or adminis-
trative action, such additional costs are reportable
under subsection (d)(2)(C) above.

Leo Ziffren, Esq.
August 11, 1982
Page Five


     (3) Dues or similar payments made by any
person for membership in a bona fide association,
including any federation, confederation or trade,
labor or membership organization, some portion of
which is used to influence legislative or adminis-
trative action, are not payments to influence
legislative or administrative action.

     (g) Applicability of Section 86108(b). For the
purpose of determining whether Section 86108(b) is
applicable to any person, all payments made during any
calendar quarter which are required to be reported by
this regulation shall be combined and a report shall be
filed if the total is $2,500 or more. If, however, the
only reportable payments made are activity expenses
within the meaning of subsection (c) above, no report
shall be required.

     Thus, the company must account for its payments to employees
who spend 10 percent or more of their compensated time in any
month preparing telegrams, etc., and if this, plus all the other
expenses described above, equals or exceeds $2,500 for the
period of July 1, 1982, through September 30, 1982, then it must
file a Form 650 report (forms enclosed).

     Section 86109 details the information required to be re-
ported on Form 650. Pursuant to Section 86110, the report
(if required) for the period of July 1, 1982 to September 30,
1982, is required to be filed with the Secretary of State's
Office2/ postmarked no later than October 31, 1982; however,
since the deadline falls on a Sunday, compliance may be achieved
by posting the report no later than Monday, November 1, 1982.3/

     As your client's situation develops further and the
extent of its activities and expenditures becomes clearer,
please feel free to contact our Technical Assistance and Analysis
(TA&A) staff with any further questions. Once your client has
determined that it has a filing/reporting obligation, you or


_________________________

     2/ Section 86111 requires the original and one copy to
be filed with the Secretary of State's Office.

     3/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 13116(a).

Leo Ziffren, Esq.
August 11, 1982
Page Six


they should feel free to contact our TA&A staff again for any
assistance needed in properly filling out the Form 650. You
may reach Lynn Montgomery or Jeanne Pritchard of our TA&A staff
at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosures

sections.question: What filing and reporting obligations would arise if a company reimbursed its employees for the cost of telegrams urging specific legislative action? Subsequently, what reporting obligations may occur if the company expends more than $2,500 in this calendar quarter in supporting this attempt to influence legislative action?
sections.conclusion: The company must account for its payments to employees who spend 10 percent or more of their compensated time in any month preparing telegrams, etc., and if this, plus all other expenses described, equals or exceeds $2,500 for the period of July 1, 1982, through September 30, 1982, then it must file a Form 650 report. The report (if required) for the period of July 1, 1982 to September 30, 1982, is required to be filed with the Secretary of State's Office postmarked no later than October 31, 1982 (or November 1, 1982, since the deadline falls on a Sunday). The company has no filing or reporting obligations unless it meets the $2,500 threshold for influencing legislative action in a calendar quarter, since it does not employ a lobbyist.
sections.question_synthetic: A company seeking to reimburse employees for sending telegrams to legislators regarding pending legislation asks what filing and reporting obligations apply under the Political Reform Act, particularly when expenditures may exceed $2,500 in a calendar quarter.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The company must file a Form 650 report if its aggregate reportable payments (including employee compensation for legislative influence activities and related expenses) equal or exceed $2,500 in the July-September 1982 quarter, with the report due by November 1, 1982.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has clear Q/C structure across multiple pages. Identified as telephone advice request letter (A-82-145). Advice is detailed with regulatory citations and examples. High confidence in extraction; minor uncertainty only regarding whether the narrative 'facts' section constitutes the verbatim question (which appears to be FPPC's summary of the phone conversation rather than a formal written question).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What filing and reporting obligations would arise if a company reimbursed its employees for the cost of telegrams urging specific legislative action? Subsequently, what reporting obligations may occur if the company expends more than $2,500 in this calendar quarter in supporting this attempt to influence legislative action?

CONCLUSION: The company must account for its payments to employees who spend 10 percent or more of their compensated time in any month preparing telegrams, etc., and if this, plus all other expenses described, equals or exceeds $2,500 for the period of July 1, 1982, through September 30, 1982, then it must file a Form 650 report. The report (if required) for the period of July 1, 1982 to September 30, 1982, is required to be filed with the Secretary of State's Office postmarked no later than October 31, 1982 (or November 1, 1982, since the deadline falls on a Sunday). The company has no filing or reporting obligations unless it meets the $2,500 threshold for influencing legislative action in a calendar quarter, since it does not employ a lobbyist.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises a company that reimbursements to employees for telegrams to legislators constitute 'payments to influence legislative action' that count toward the $2,500 quarterly reporting threshold. If the company's total qualifying expenditures reach $2,500 or more in the July-September 1982 quarter, it must file a Form 650 report by November 1, 1982.
